title: Informed Consent for an 80-Year-Old with Advanced Dementia and a Hip Fracture Who LACKS Decisional Capacity and Has NO Advance Directive or Named Health-Care Proxy: Capacity vs Competence, the Elements of Valid Consent and the NURSE'S Witness (NOT Obtain) Role, the Statutory SURROGATE Hierarchy, Substituted Judgment vs Best Interest, Emergency (Implied) Consent, and Ethics/Guardianship as a Last Resort
subtitle: When an older adult needs a treatment decision but may not be able to make it, the nurse must understand DECISIONAL CAPACITY and INFORMED CONSENT. CAPACITY is a CLINICAL, DECISION-SPECIFIC, and PRESUMED determination — can the person UNDERSTAND the relevant information, APPRECIATE how it applies to them, REASON about the options, and COMMUNICATE a stable CHOICE? It can fluctuate and is specific to the decision at hand. COMPETENCE, by contrast, is a GLOBAL LEGAL status determined by a COURT (the clinical team assesses capacity; only a court declares incompetence). Before concluding a patient has DURABLE incapacity, the nurse SCREENS FOR and OPTIMIZES REVERSIBLE contributors — DELIRIUM (use CAM), infection (e.g., UTI), PAIN, sedating MEDICATIONS, metabolic/electrolyte derangements, hypoxia, and sensory deficits (hearing aids/glasses) — and reassesses, because an unwise choice, old age, a dementia label, or simple disagreement do NOT by themselves equal incapacity. ELEMENTS of VALID INFORMED CONSENT: (1) the decision-maker has CAPACITY and the AUTHORITY to decide; (2) the decision is VOLUNTARY (free of coercion); (3) adequate DISCLOSURE — the diagnosis, the NATURE and PURPOSE of the proposed procedure, the material RISKS and BENEFITS, the ALTERNATIVES (including the option of NO treatment) and their consequences; (4) the decision-maker UNDERSTANDS; and (5) CONSENT is given. The PROVIDER who will perform the procedure is responsible for the DISCLOSURE and for OBTAINING informed consent; the NURSE'S role is to WITNESS the signature, confirm the consent appears VOLUNTARY and that the signer appears to UNDERSTAND, reinforce/clarify teaching, ADVOCATE, and NOTIFY the provider if the patient/surrogate has questions or does not understand — the nurse does NOT provide the disclosure for or obtain consent on behalf of the provider, and never signs FOR the patient. WHEN THE PATIENT LACKS CAPACITY: first look for an ADVANCE DIRECTIVE (living will) or a designated HEALTH-CARE PROXY / durable power of attorney for health care; a directive completed while the patient HAD capacity is binding. If there is NO advance directive and NO named proxy (as here), a SURROGATE is identified per the STATE SURROGATE-CONSENT STATUTE HIERARCHY — a typical order is: a court-appointed GUARDIAN (if one exists) → SPOUSE → ADULT CHILDREN (often by majority) → PARENTS → ADULT SIBLINGS → other relatives or a close friend who knows the patient's values. The surrogate decides using the SUBSTITUTED-JUDGMENT standard (what the PATIENT would have wanted, based on previously expressed values, beliefs, and statements) and, only when those wishes are unknown, the BEST-INTEREST standard (the option that best promotes the patient's welfare). EMERGENCY (IMPLIED) CONSENT: in an IMMEDIATE life- or limb-threatening EMERGENCY when no surrogate is reachable, treatment needed to prevent death or serious harm may proceed under implied consent (a reasonable person would consent) — but this does NOT apply to a non-emergent decision when a surrogate IS available (as with this semi-urgent hip repair). WHEN SURROGATES CONFLICT, no surrogate exists (the 'unrepresented/unbefriended' patient), or the decision is ethically complex → obtain an ETHICS CONSULT, and pursue COURT-APPOINTED GUARDIANSHIP (guardian of the PERSON for health decisions; a conservator/guardian of the ESTATE for finances) only as a LAST RESORT and least-restrictive option. Throughout, the patient is INVOLVED to the extent possible (simple explanations, ASSENT, daily preferences) and her DIGNITY and rights are protected; CODE STATUS is established with the surrogate. NURSING ROLE: assess capacity and optimize reversible causes; identify the correct decision-maker (advance directive/proxy, then the surrogate hierarchy); ensure the PROVIDER obtains valid informed consent while the nurse witnesses, verifies understanding, and advocates; support substituted judgment then best interest; involve the patient and protect dignity; and escalate to ethics/guardianship only when truly needed.

patient.name: Eleanor V.
patient.code: Full code pending surrogate discussion; goal = ensure a valid, ethically and legally sound treatment decision for a patient who lacks capacity — identify the correct surrogate decision-maker, obtain proper informed consent, apply substituted judgment, and protect the patient's dignity and involvement
patient.admitDate: An 80-year-old woman with ADVANCED dementia (lives in a memory-care facility, baseline disoriented, requires full assistance) is admitted after a fall with a displaced femoral-neck HIP FRACTURE. Orthopedics recommends surgical fixation/hemiarthroplasty — important and semi-urgent, but NOT an immediate life-threatening emergency. On assessment she cannot state where she is, does not understand the proposed surgery or its risks, and cannot reason about or communicate a stable choice — she LACKS decisional capacity for this decision. There is NO advance directive and NO designated health-care proxy on file. She has two adult children (a son and a daughter). The team must identify the appropriate surrogate decision-maker, ensure valid informed consent, and guide the decision by what Eleanor would have wanted, while screening for reversible contributors (pain, delirium, possible UTI) and protecting her dignity.
patient.room: Orthopedic/surgical unit; interdisciplinary team (orthopedic surgeon, hospitalist/geriatrics, bedside RN, case management/social work, ethics consult available, chaplain); family (son and daughter) being contacted; capacity assessment, surrogate identification, and informed-consent process in progress

vitals.temp: 37.5°C; displaced femoral-neck hip fracture with pain on movement; baseline advanced dementia with acute disorientation; cannot describe the injury or proposed surgery — apparent lack of decisional capacity; mild temperature elevation prompts screening for a reversible contributor (e.g., UTI/delirium)

37.3°C; formal capacity assessment (understand/appreciate/reason/communicate a choice) — she cannot; CAM screen for DELIRIUM; treat/optimize reversible contributors (pain control, evaluate possible UTI, review sedating meds, hearing aids/glasses) and reassess — advanced dementia indicates durable incapacity for this complex decision

37.1°C; NO advance directive and NO named health-care proxy on file → identify a SURROGATE per the state statutory hierarchy (guardian → spouse → adult children → parents → siblings → close other); she is widowed, so her two ADULT CHILDREN are next — engage both

37.0°C; the ORTHOPEDIC SURGEON discloses the diagnosis, the procedure's nature/purpose, material risks/benefits, and alternatives (including no surgery) to the surrogate and obtains consent; the NURSE WITNESSES the signature, confirms it is voluntary and that the surrogate appears to understand, and notifies the surgeon of any questions

vitals.time: Substituted judgment + patient involvement + code status
vitals.temp: 37.0°C; surrogate decides by SUBSTITUTED JUDGMENT (what Eleanor would have wanted from her known values), then best interest if unknown; the patient is INVOLVED to the extent possible (simple explanation, comfort, assent, dignity); CODE STATUS established with the surrogate; ethics consult available if conflict arises

37.0°C; surgery proceeded with valid surrogate informed consent; pain controlled, delirium-prevention measures in place, dignity preserved, family aligned with Eleanor's values; documentation of capacity assessment, decision-maker, consent, and code status complete

labs.n: PRESENTATION + capacity vs competence + assessing capacity/reversible causes
labs.v: Eleanor V., an 80-year-old with ADVANCED dementia, is admitted with a displaced femoral-neck HIP FRACTURE for which orthopedics recommends surgical fixation — important and semi-urgent, but NOT an immediate life-threatening emergency. The first question is whether she can make this decision herself. DECISIONAL CAPACITY is a CLINICAL, DECISION-SPECIFIC, and PRESUMED determination assessed by the team: can she UNDERSTAND the relevant information, APPRECIATE how it applies to her, REASON among the options, and COMMUNICATE a stable CHOICE? Here she cannot state where she is or grasp the proposed surgery or its risks, so she LACKS capacity for this decision. This differs from COMPETENCE, a GLOBAL LEGAL status that only a COURT can determine. Critically, before concluding she has DURABLE incapacity, the nurse SCREENS FOR and OPTIMIZES REVERSIBLE contributors: DELIRIUM (CAM), infection (her mild fever prompts a UTI evaluation), PAIN, sedating MEDICATIONS, metabolic/electrolyte issues, hypoxia, and missing sensory aids (hearing aids/glasses) — and then reassesses. An unwise choice, advanced age, a dementia label, or mere disagreement do NOT by themselves equal incapacity; but in advanced dementia, after optimizing reversible factors, durable incapacity for a complex surgical decision is expected. Establishing the capacity determination correctly is the foundation for everything that follows.
labs.r: CAPACITY = clinical, decision-SPECIFIC, presumed (understand/appreciate/reason/communicate a choice); fluctuates. COMPETENCE = global LEGAL status decided by a COURT. Before declaring durable incapacity, screen/optimize REVERSIBLE causes (delirium/CAM, infection/UTI, pain, sedating meds, metabolic, sensory aids) + reassess. Unwise choice/old age/dementia label/disagreement ≠ incapacity. RN role: assess capacity + rule out reversible contributors.

labs.n: Elements of valid informed consent + the NURSE'S role (witness, NOT obtain)
labs.v: VALID INFORMED CONSENT has defined ELEMENTS: (1) the decision-maker has CAPACITY and the legal AUTHORITY to decide; (2) the decision is VOLUNTARY — free of coercion or undue pressure; (3) adequate DISCLOSURE — the diagnosis, the NATURE and PURPOSE of the proposed procedure, the material RISKS and BENEFITS, and the ALTERNATIVES (including the option of NO treatment) with their consequences; (4) the decision-maker UNDERSTANDS the information; and (5) CONSENT is given (or refused). RESPONSIBILITY: the PROVIDER who will perform the procedure (here the surgeon) is legally responsible for the DISCLOSURE and for OBTAINING informed consent. The NURSE'S ROLE is distinct and important but limited: WITNESS the signature; confirm the consent appears VOLUNTARY and that the signer appears to UNDERSTAND; reinforce and clarify teaching within the nurse's scope; serve as the patient's ADVOCATE; and NOTIFY the provider if the patient/surrogate has unanswered questions, seems not to understand, or wishes to change/withdraw the decision (consent can be withdrawn at any time). The nurse does NOT deliver the procedure-specific disclosure for the provider, does NOT obtain consent on the provider's behalf, and NEVER signs the form FOR the patient or surrogate. If the signer does not understand, the nurse stops the process and calls the provider back — a signature without understanding is not valid consent.
labs.r: Valid informed consent = capable/authorized decision-maker + VOLUNTARY + DISCLOSURE (diagnosis, procedure nature/purpose, risks/benefits, ALTERNATIVES incl. no treatment) + UNDERSTANDING + consent. The PROVIDER obtains consent (disclosure is their duty). NURSE = WITNESS signature + confirm voluntary/understanding + reinforce + advocate + notify provider of questions/withdrawal. Nurse NEVER provides the disclosure for the provider or signs FOR the patient. RN role: witness + verify + advocate, not obtain.

labs.n: No advance directive/proxy → surrogate hierarchy + substituted judgment vs best interest + emergency consent
labs.v: Because Eleanor lacks capacity, the team looks for an ADVANCE DIRECTIVE (living will) or a designated HEALTH-CARE PROXY / durable power of attorney for health care — a directive made while she HAD capacity would be binding. There is NONE on file. With no advance directive and no named proxy, a SURROGATE is identified per the STATE SURROGATE-CONSENT STATUTE HIERARCHY; a typical order is: a court-appointed GUARDIAN (if any) → SPOUSE → ADULT CHILDREN (often by majority agreement) → PARENTS → ADULT SIBLINGS → another relative or close friend who knows her values. Eleanor is widowed, so her two ADULT CHILDREN are the surrogates and are engaged together. The surrogate must decide by the SUBSTITUTED-JUDGMENT standard — choosing what ELEANOR would have wanted based on her previously expressed values, beliefs, religious/cultural views, and prior statements — and only when her wishes are truly unknown does the surrogate use the BEST-INTEREST standard (the option that best promotes her welfare, weighing benefits, burdens, and quality of life). The EMERGENCY (IMPLIED) CONSENT exception allows treatment to proceed WITHOUT formal consent ONLY in an IMMEDIATE life- or limb-threatening emergency when no surrogate is reachable, because a reasonable person would consent to life-saving care — but this hip repair is semi-urgent and a surrogate IS available, so emergency consent does NOT apply and proper surrogate consent is required before surgery.
labs.r: No capacity → check advance directive/health-care proxy (binding if made with capacity). NONE → SURROGATE per statutory HIERARCHY: guardian → spouse → adult children → parents → siblings → close other. Surrogate uses SUBSTITUTED JUDGMENT (what the PATIENT would want) → BEST INTEREST only if wishes unknown. EMERGENCY (IMPLIED) consent ONLY for immediate life-/limb-threatening emergency with no surrogate reachable — NOT for a non-emergent decision when a surrogate is available. RN role: find the right decision-maker + support substituted judgment.

labs.n: Conflict / no surrogate → ethics + guardianship (last resort); patient involvement + dignity
labs.v: Surrogate decision-making is not always straightforward. If the adult children DISAGREE with each other, if NO surrogate exists or can be found (the 'UNREPRESENTED' or 'unbefriended' patient), or if the decision is ETHICALLY COMPLEX (e.g., high-risk surgery vs comfort care, conflicting values), the team obtains an ETHICS CONSULT to facilitate a values-based, structured discussion rather than forcing a decision or letting one family member dominate. COURT-APPOINTED GUARDIANSHIP/CONSERVATORSHIP is pursued only as a LAST RESORT and least-restrictive option when there is no appropriate surrogate or unresolvable conflict — a GUARDIAN OF THE PERSON makes health-care and personal decisions, while a CONSERVATOR/GUARDIAN OF THE ESTATE manages finances; guardianship is a significant restriction of autonomy and should not be the first move. Throughout, the patient is INVOLVED to the extent possible: give simple, honest explanations at her level, seek her ASSENT and honor her expressed daily preferences (comfort, food, music, presence of loved ones), and protect her DIGNITY and remaining rights — advanced dementia does NOT erase her personhood. CODE STATUS and goals of care are established with the surrogate using substituted judgment. The bedside nurse advocates for Eleanor, keeps her comfortable (pain control, delirium prevention), documents the capacity assessment, the identified decision-maker, the consent, and code status, and ensures the process is transparent and patient-centered.
labs.r: Surrogate conflict / no surrogate (unrepresented) / ethically complex → ETHICS CONSULT; court GUARDIANSHIP only as LAST RESORT/least-restrictive (guardian of PERSON = health/personal; conservator/guardian of ESTATE = finances). Always INVOLVE the patient to the extent possible (simple explanation, assent, daily preferences, dignity — dementia ≠ loss of personhood). Establish code status via substituted judgment. RN role: advocate, involve, escalate appropriately, document.

labs.n: Pertinent NEGATIVES + protective factors + nursing summary
labs.v: PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice): the team did NOT conclude durable incapacity without first screening/optimizing reversible contributors (delirium, UTI, pain, sedating meds) and reassessing; the nurse did NOT sign the consent FOR the patient and did NOT provide the procedure-specific disclosure for or obtain consent on behalf of the surgeon; the team did NOT proceed with the NON-emergent surgery without consent simply because the patient 'can't decide,' and did NOT misapply the emergency/implied-consent exception when a surrogate was available; staff did NOT coerce or pressure the surrogate to sign quickly to keep the OR schedule; the surrogate was guided to use SUBSTITUTED JUDGMENT (what Eleanor would want) rather than the surrogate's own preference; and the patient was NOT excluded or stripped of dignity/rights because she has dementia. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: a careful capacity assessment with reversible-cause optimization; correct identification of the decision-maker via the statutory hierarchy; valid provider-obtained informed consent witnessed and verified by the nurse; decisions grounded in the patient's known values; patient involvement and dignity preserved; availability of an ethics consult for conflict; and thorough documentation. EXPECTED outcome: a valid, ethically and legally sound decision is reached, the appropriate surgery proceeds with proper consent (or is declined per the patient's values), the patient is kept comfortable and involved, and family is aligned with what Eleanor would have wanted. NURSING ROLE: assess capacity and optimize reversible causes; identify the correct decision-maker (advance directive/proxy, then the surrogate hierarchy); ensure the PROVIDER obtains valid informed consent while the nurse witnesses, verifies understanding, and advocates; support substituted judgment then best interest; involve the patient and protect dignity; establish code status; escalate to ethics/guardianship only when truly needed; and document the process.
labs.r: NEGATIVES (correct practice): didn't skip reversible-cause workup, nurse didn't sign FOR patient or obtain the disclosure, didn't operate without consent on a non-emergency, didn't misuse emergency consent, didn't coerce the surrogate, didn't substitute the surrogate's own wishes, didn't exclude/demean the patient. PROTECTIVE: capacity assessment + reversible optimization, correct surrogate per hierarchy, provider-obtained/nurse-witnessed consent, substituted judgment, patient involvement/dignity, ethics consult availability, documentation. RN role: assess capacity + right decision-maker + witness/verify consent + advocate + involve + document.

nursesNote: Orthopedic/surgical unit — Eleanor V., 80, ADVANCED dementia (memory-care, baseline disoriented, full assistance), admitted after a fall with a displaced femoral-neck HIP FRACTURE; orthopedics recommends surgical fixation/hemiarthroplasty — important + semi-urgent but NOT an immediate life-threatening emergency. CAPACITY: clinical, decision-specific, presumed (understand/appreciate/reason/communicate a choice) — she CANNOT (doesn't know where she is, can't grasp the surgery/risks) → LACKS capacity for this decision; COMPETENCE = global LEGAL status only a COURT decides. Before concluding DURABLE incapacity: screen/optimize REVERSIBLE contributors — CAM for delirium, evaluate possible UTI (mild fever 37.5), pain control, review sedating meds, hearing aids/glasses — and reassess; advanced dementia → durable incapacity for this complex decision. (Unwise choice/age/dementia label/disagreement ≠ incapacity.) INFORMED CONSENT elements: capable/authorized decision-maker + VOLUNTARY + DISCLOSURE (diagnosis, procedure nature/purpose, risks/benefits, ALTERNATIVES incl. no treatment) + UNDERSTANDING + consent. The SURGEON (provider) obtains consent/disclosure; NURSE role = WITNESS signature + confirm voluntary/apparent understanding + reinforce teaching + ADVOCATE + notify provider of questions/withdrawal (consent withdrawable anytime). Nurse NEVER provides the disclosure for the provider or signs FOR the patient; if signer doesn't understand → stop + call provider back. DECISION-MAKER: NO advance directive + NO named proxy on file → SURROGATE per state statutory HIERARCHY (guardian → spouse → adult children → parents → siblings → close other); widowed → her two ADULT CHILDREN engaged together. Surrogate uses SUBSTITUTED JUDGMENT (what ELEANOR would want from known values) → BEST INTEREST only if wishes unknown. EMERGENCY (IMPLIED) consent ONLY for immediate life-/limb-threatening emergency with no surrogate reachable — does NOT apply here (semi-urgent + surrogate available) → proper surrogate consent required first. CONFLICT/no surrogate/complexity → ETHICS CONSULT; court GUARDIANSHIP only LAST RESORT (guardian of PERSON = health/personal; conservator/estate = finances). INVOLVE the patient to extent possible (simple explanation, assent, daily preferences, comfort, DIGNITY — dementia ≠ loss of personhood); establish CODE STATUS with surrogate. NEGATIVES (correct practice): didn't skip reversible-cause workup, nurse didn't sign FOR her or obtain the disclosure, didn't operate without consent on a non-emergency, didn't misuse emergency consent, didn't coerce the surrogate, didn't impose the surrogate's own wishes, didn't exclude/demean her. Pain controlled + delirium-prevention measures; documented capacity assessment, decision-maker, consent, and code status.
providerOrders: ADMIT: orthopedic/surgical unit; 80-year-old with advanced dementia and a displaced femoral-neck hip fracture; orthopedic surgery (fixation/hemiarthroplasty) recommended — semi-urgent, not an immediate life-threatening emergency
Assess capacity for this decision (understand/appreciate/reason/communicate a choice); document findings
Screen for and optimize reversible contributors: CAM for delirium, evaluate for UTI/infection, control pain, review sedating medications, ensure hearing aids/glasses; reassess capacity
Search the record for an advance directive (living will) or designated health-care proxy/durable power of attorney for health care
If none, identify the surrogate per the state statutory hierarchy (guardian → spouse → adult children → parents → siblings → close other); engage both adult children
Surgeon to disclose diagnosis, procedure nature/purpose, material risks/benefits, and alternatives (including no surgery) and obtain consent from the surrogate
RN to witness the signature, confirm it is voluntary and that the surrogate appears to understand, reinforce teaching, and notify the surgeon of any questions or withdrawal
Guide the surrogate to use substituted judgment (what the patient would have wanted) and, if her wishes are unknown, the best-interest standard
Establish code status/goals of care with the surrogate
Involve the patient to the extent possible (simple explanations, assent, honor daily preferences), protect dignity and rights; provide pain control and delirium-prevention measures
Obtain an ethics consult if surrogates disagree, no surrogate is available, or the decision is ethically complex; pursue court-appointed guardianship only as a last resort
DOCUMENTATION: capacity assessment, identified decision-maker, informed-consent process, code status, and patient involvement

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters for this consent decision?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings/considerations that are clinically significant.

opts.text: The patient lacks decisional capacity for this surgical decision — she cannot understand, appreciate, reason about, or communicate a stable choice due to advanced dementia
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — establishing the capacity determination is the foundation of the consent process. RN role: assess decision-specific capacity.

opts.text: Screen for and optimize reversible contributors to impaired cognition (delirium/CAM, possible UTI, pain, sedating meds, sensory aids) and reassess before concluding durable incapacity
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — reversible causes must be excluded/optimized; capacity is presumed and decision-specific. RN role: rule out reversible contributors.

opts.text: There is no advance directive and no named health-care proxy → a surrogate must be identified per the state statutory hierarchy
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the correct decision-maker must be identified when capacity is lacking. RN role: identify the surrogate.

opts.text: The hip repair is semi-urgent but not an immediate life-threatening emergency, and a surrogate is available → proper surrogate informed consent is required (not emergency/implied consent)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — emergency consent does not apply when the situation is non-emergent and a surrogate exists. RN role: require proper consent.

opts.text: The surgeon must obtain informed consent (disclosure); the nurse's role is to witness the signature, confirm voluntariness/understanding, advocate, and notify the provider of questions
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the provider obtains consent; the nurse witnesses and verifies. RN role: witness/verify, not obtain.

opts.text: Guide the decision by substituted judgment (Eleanor's known values) and involve the patient to the extent possible, protecting her dignity
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — substituted judgment and patient involvement/dignity govern the decision. RN role: support the patient's voice and values.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA — document for the perioperative record
opts.rat: Allergy documentation; routine before surgery. Not the central cue.

opts.text: Because the patient 'can't decide,' the nurse should sign the consent form on her behalf and obtain the consent herself
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. The nurse never signs for the patient and does not obtain the provider's informed consent; an authorized surrogate decides, the surgeon discloses/obtains consent, and the nurse witnesses.

opts.text: Proceed straight to surgery under emergency/implied consent even though it is not an immediate life-threatening emergency and a surrogate is available
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Emergency/implied consent applies only to immediate life-/limb-threatening emergencies with no surrogate reachable; here proper surrogate consent must be obtained first.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What consent and capacity principles apply?

opts.text: Capacity is a clinical, decision-specific, presumed determination (understand/appreciate/reason/communicate a choice), while competence is a global legal status decided by a court
opts.rat: CORRECT — the capacity-vs-competence distinction. Foundation for the determination.

opts.text: Valid informed consent requires an authorized decision-maker with capacity, voluntariness, disclosure (diagnosis, procedure, risks/benefits, alternatives including no treatment), understanding, and consent
opts.rat: CORRECT — the elements of valid informed consent. Foundation for a lawful decision.

opts.text: The provider performing the procedure obtains informed consent; the nurse witnesses the signature, verifies understanding, advocates, and notifies the provider of concerns
opts.rat: CORRECT — division of responsibility for consent. Foundation for the nurse's role.

opts.text: With no advance directive or proxy, a surrogate is identified per the statutory hierarchy (guardian → spouse → adult children → parents → siblings) and decides by substituted judgment, then best interest
opts.rat: CORRECT — surrogate identification and decision standards. Foundation for who decides and how.

opts.text: Emergency (implied) consent permits treatment only in an immediate life-/limb-threatening emergency when no surrogate is reachable
opts.rat: CORRECT — the scope of the emergency-consent exception. Foundation for when it applies.

opts.text: Surrogate conflict, no available surrogate, or ethically complex decisions warrant an ethics consult, with court guardianship only as a last resort
opts.rat: CORRECT — escalation pathway. Foundation for resolving difficult cases.

opts.text: A nurse may legally provide the procedure-specific disclosure and obtain informed consent for the surgeon's operation
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Disclosure and obtaining consent are the provider's legal responsibility; the nurse witnesses, verifies, and advocates but does not obtain the consent.

opts.text: An advanced-dementia diagnosis alone means the patient has no rights and need not be involved or informed in any way
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Dementia does not erase personhood or rights; the patient is involved to the extent possible (simple explanation, assent, preferences) and her dignity is protected.

opts.text: Assess the patient's decisional capacity for this decision and optimize/rule out reversible contributors (delirium, infection, pain, sedating meds)
opts.rat: #1 — Foundational. Whether she can decide herself determines everything else. RN role: capacity assessment + reversible-cause optimization.

opts.text: Determine the correct decision-maker — check for an advance directive/proxy, then apply the statutory surrogate hierarchy
opts.rat: #2 — High. Identifying the authorized decision-maker must precede consent. RN role: identify the decision-maker.

opts.text: Ensure the provider obtains valid informed consent from the surrogate (disclosure, voluntariness, understanding); the nurse witnesses, verifies, and advocates
opts.rat: #3 — Core. Valid consent legitimizes the procedure. RN role: witness/verify the consent process.

opts.text: Guide decisions by substituted judgment (the patient's known values), then best interest, and involve the patient to the extent possible
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. The decision standard and patient involvement ensure the choice reflects the patient. RN role: support substituted judgment + involvement.

opts.text: Escalate to an ethics consult/guardianship only if there is conflict, no surrogate, or complexity; establish code status and document the process
opts.rat: #5 — As needed/ongoing. Escalation is reserved for difficult cases; documentation and code status complete the process. RN role: escalate appropriately + document.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this consent decision.

opts.text: Assess capacity and screen for/optimize reversible causes of impaired cognition (delirium, UTI, pain, sedating meds) before concluding durable incapacity
opts.rat: Correct capacity workup. RN role: assess capacity + optimize reversible causes.

opts.text: Check for an advance directive/health-care proxy; finding none, identify the surrogate per the statutory hierarchy (here, the adult children)
opts.rat: Correct identification of the authorized decision-maker. RN role: identify the surrogate.

opts.text: Have the surgeon disclose the diagnosis, procedure, risks/benefits, and alternatives to the surrogate and obtain consent; the nurse witnesses and verifies understanding
opts.rat: Correct division of the consent process. RN role: witness + verify, provider obtains.

opts.text: Support the surrogate in using substituted judgment (what Eleanor would want) based on her known values
opts.rat: Correct decision standard. RN role: guide substituted judgment.

opts.text: Involve the patient to the extent possible (simple explanation, comfort, assent, honoring daily preferences) and protect her dignity
opts.rat: Patient-centered, dignity-preserving care. RN role: involve the patient + protect dignity.

opts.text: Obtain an ethics consult if the children disagree or no surrogate is available; pursue guardianship only as a last resort
opts.rat: Appropriate escalation for conflict/no surrogate. RN role: escalate to ethics; guardianship as last resort.

opts.text: Have the nurse sign or obtain the informed consent on the patient's behalf
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — the nurse never signs for the patient or obtains the provider's consent; an authorized surrogate decides and the surgeon obtains consent.

opts.text: Proceed with the non-emergent surgery without any consent because the patient 'can't decide'
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — a non-emergent procedure requires valid surrogate consent; operating without it violates the patient's rights and the law.

opts.text: Pressure or rush the hesitant surrogate into consenting quickly to keep the OR schedule
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — consent must be voluntary and informed; coercion invalidates it. The surrogate must have time to understand and ask questions.

opts.text: Exclude the patient entirely and withhold all information and involvement because she has dementia
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — dementia does not remove personhood; involve her to the extent possible (simple explanation, assent, preferences) and protect her dignity.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Assess capacity and optimize/rule out reversible contributors; confirm she lacks capacity for this decision
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Establishing the capacity determination (after optimizing reversible causes) drives the entire process. RN role: capacity assessment.

opts.text: Check for an advance directive/proxy; finding none, identify the appropriate surrogate per the statutory hierarchy
opts.rat: #2 — Second. The authorized decision-maker must be identified before consent can be obtained. RN role: identify the surrogate.

opts.text: Arrange for the surgeon to disclose and obtain informed consent from the surrogate; witness the signature and verify voluntariness/understanding
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Valid, provider-obtained consent (nurse-witnessed) legitimizes the procedure. RN role: facilitate + witness + verify.

opts.text: Support substituted judgment and involve the patient to the extent possible; establish code status with the surrogate
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. The decision standard, patient involvement, and code status ensure a patient-centered choice. RN role: substituted judgment + involvement + code status.

opts.text: If conflict, no surrogate, or complexity arises, obtain an ethics consult (guardianship only as a last resort); document the process
opts.rat: #5 — As needed/closing. Escalate difficult cases appropriately and document the capacity assessment, decision-maker, consent, and code status. RN role: escalate + document.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each action, classify as APPROPRIATE or INAPPROPRIATE.

opts.text: The nurse assessed capacity, treated a contributing UTI/delirium and pain, and reassessed before concluding the patient had durable incapacity
opts.rat: Capacity is presumed and decision-specific; optimizing reversible causes before concluding incapacity is correct practice.

opts.text: With no advance directive or proxy, the team identified the surrogate per the statutory hierarchy, and the agreeing adult children served as decision-makers
opts.rat: Correct surrogate identification when no directive/proxy exists. Correct practice.

opts.text: The surgeon disclosed the risks, benefits, and alternatives to the surrogate and obtained consent; the nurse witnessed the signature and confirmed understanding
opts.rat: Proper division of the consent process with nurse verification. Correct practice.

opts.text: The surrogate used substituted judgment based on Eleanor's known values, and the patient was involved with simple explanations and kept comfortable
opts.rat: Substituted judgment plus patient involvement and dignity. Correct practice.

opts.text: When the son and daughter disagreed about proceeding, the team obtained an ethics consult rather than forcing a decision
opts.rat: Ethics consultation is the right step for surrogate conflict. Correct practice.

opts.text: The nurse signed the consent form for the patient because 'she can't decide'
opts.rat: ⚠️ The nurse never signs for the patient; an authorized surrogate decides and the surgeon obtains consent. Improper practice.

opts.text: The team proceeded with the non-emergent hip surgery with no consent, reasoning that the patient lacked capacity
opts.rat: ⚠️ A non-emergent procedure requires valid surrogate consent; operating without it violates the patient's rights. Improper practice.

opts.text: Staff pressured the hesitant daughter into signing quickly to avoid delaying the OR schedule
opts.rat: ⚠️ Consent must be voluntary and informed; coercion invalidates it. Improper practice.

opts.text: The patient was told nothing and excluded from all interaction because she has dementia
opts.rat: ⚠️ Dementia does not remove personhood; the patient must be involved to the extent possible and her dignity protected. Improper practice.

opts.text: In a separate scenario, a truly life-threatening emergency arose with no surrogate reachable, and the team treated under emergency (implied) consent to prevent serious harm
opts.rat: Emergency/implied consent correctly applies to an immediate life-threatening emergency when no surrogate is reachable. Correct practice.

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Management of Care, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
